caveats: a: BLS does not publish the cited occupational evidence specifically for 424720.
a: Firm business models range from desk-based fuel marketers to route-heavy packaged-product distributors.
a: The injected compensation ratio uses high and volatile petroleum receipts, so it should not be read as a stable task share.
rho: No representative longitudinal AI implementation study for 424720 was found.
rho: Technical feasibility may exceed realized adoption at owner-operated firms with legacy systems.
e: The Census definition identifies establishment activity but not recurring-revenue quality or firm transferability.
e: The injected margin bridge may misclassify EBITDA band because petroleum revenue and gross profit vary greatly with commodity prices.
s5: No industry-specific owner-age or succession-rate evidence was found.
s5: Broad marketplace transactions skew smaller than the target EBITDA band and omit private strategic deals.
q: No causal pass-through study for AI savings in petroleum wholesaling was found.
q: Nominal sales and gross margin can be dominated by fuel-price changes rather than retained productivity.
d5: National product supplied is not the same as service quantity handled by independent non-terminal wholesalers.
d5: Weather materially affects heating oil and propane, while commodity-price changes distort nominal industry sales.
d5: Exports may bypass the screened population.
o: Physical delivery may remain operator-required while migrating to a carrier outside NAICS 424720.
o: Requirements for bottled LP gas do not apply uniformly across gasoline, fuel oil, and lubricants.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B distribution to retailers, commercial fleets, industrial and agricultural users, and other enterprises, earning a product spread and sometimes delivery, packaging, credit, equipment, route, or account-service economics; prices often reference volatile wholesale fuel markets.
lens.excluded_activities: Bulk stations and terminals, integrated producer or refinery sales branches, retail-only fuel or propane dealers, commission-only agents, captive internal distributors, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent US lower-middle-market merchant wholesalers repeatedly sourcing, selling, coordinating, and where applicable packaging or delivering gasoline, fuel oil, lubricants and grease, bottled liquefied petroleum gas, and other petroleum products without operating a qualifying bulk liquid storage station or terminal.

a.bridge: BLS reports a broader nondurable-wholesale occupation mix containing sales representatives, drivers, material movers, and shipping clerks; separate BLS pages describe wholesale sales and delivery/driver-sales work nationwide. I translate these broader occupations into 424720 tasks: quoting, price-file updates, order intake, route and dispatch planning, credit review, invoice reconciliation, account research, replenishment suggestions, and routine customer support are exposed, while driving, cylinder or package handling, on-site transfer, vehicle inspection, and relationship selling are much less exposed. The proxy can overstate exposure where driver payroll dominates and understate it in desk-based fuel marketers.
caveats: BLS does not publish the cited occupational evidence specifically for 424720.
Firm business models range from desk-based fuel marketers to route-heavy packaged-product distributors.
The injected compensation ratio uses high and volatile petroleum receipts, so it should not be read as a stable task share.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted 424720 occupation extract plus task-level time studies at at least 15 fuel, lubricant, and bottled-LPG wholesalers, separated by desk marketing, inside and outside sales, dispatch, accounting, warehouse, and driver roles.
a.rationale: Compared with a bulk terminal, a non-terminal petroleum wholesaler generally has more commercial, order, route, and account work relative to fixed infrastructure operation. That makes a meaningful share of tasks AI-addressable, but delivery and hazardous-product handling remain physical and customer relationships and exceptions still require people. A 28% base reflects partial exposure across a mixed sales-logistics workforce.

d5.bridge: EIA reports motor-gasoline consumption of 8.9 million barrels per day in 2025, 1% below 2024 and 4% below 2019, with further near-term decline expected; it also reports growing refined-product exports and substantial propane demand and inventory. These product quantities span markets served by 424720 but do not isolate wholesaler service volume or distinguish direct, terminal, and non-terminal channels. Gasoline trends can overstate decline for lubricant, LPG, and specialty-product distributors, while export growth can overstate opportunity for local wholesalers because much of it moves through large terminals.
caveats: National product supplied is not the same as service quantity handled by independent non-terminal wholesalers.
Weather materially affects heating oil and propane, while commodity-price changes distort nominal industry sales.
Exports may bypass the screened population.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted, product-level sales gallons and gross profit for 424720 firms from EIA prime-supplier data, Census microdata, and distributor panels, combined with five-year customer fleet, heating, industrial, agricultural, and direct-sourcing forecasts.
d5.rationale: Real demand is mixed. Efficiency and electrification weigh on gasoline and heating-oil quantity, but commercial delivery, lubricant, LPG, agricultural, fleet, backup-power, and specialty uses persist. A modestly declining base is appropriate, with the lower bound reflecting accelerated substitution and direct sourcing and the upper bound reflecting economic activity, product mix, exports, and gains by service-intensive distributors.

caveats: The Census definition identifies establishment activity but not recurring-revenue quality or firm transferability.
The injected margin bridge may misclassify EBITDA band because petroleum revenue and gross profit vary greatly with commodity prices.
e.change_evidence: A stratified sample of all firms in the injected count, linked to ownership trees, revenue by customer and product, repeat-order rate, supply contracts, route density, licenses, owner dependence, retail share, and historical gross profit excluding inventory-price effects.
e.rationale: Independent firms with repeat commercial customers, transferable supply arrangements, route or account density, and documented compliance fit the lens. Eligibility is reduced by captive or effectively captive marketers, spot traders, owner-only relationship books, retail-dominant dealers, severe customer or supplier concentration, untransferable brand or supply contracts, and firms whose normalized earnings are mostly inventory or commodity bets rather than repeat distribution service.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 424720 ($8,180,453,984) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 424720 ($802,687,684,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1137 (margins.json[42]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (Distributors) EBITDA/Sales 11.37%); revenue/employee $25,239,370 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 424720); class <5: 791 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,471,382 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $963,196 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 286 firms, avg revenue/firm $41,357,671 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,702,367 -> IN $1-10M band; class 10-19: 194 firms, avg revenue/firm $93,817,010 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $10,666,994 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 270 firms, avg revenue/firm $380,495,885 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $43,262,382 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 112 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,004,032,670 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $227,858,515 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 110 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,988,840,718 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $453,531,190 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 286

o.bridge: BLS expects online wholesale sales mostly to complement face-to-face sales and expects business demand for local delivery services to remain strong; OSHA requires a competent attendant to remain through LP-gas dispensing. These observations concern broader occupations and one regulated product rather than the five-year quantity share for 424720. I translate them into a high but not near-total need for an accountable distributor: physical delivery, credit, product responsibility, and service remain, while direct manufacturer/terminal supply, electronic self-service, third-party carriers, and customer consolidation can bypass this specific merchant-wholesaler layer. The LP-gas rule may overstate human-presence needs for other petroleum products.
caveats: Physical delivery may remain operator-required while migrating to a carrier outside NAICS 424720.
Requirements for bottled LP gas do not apply uniformly across gasoline, fuel oil, and lubricants.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement surveys and shipment data tracking direct-refiner and direct-terminal purchases, marketplace orders, third-party carrier use, drop shipment, distributor-of-record responsibility, and local route penetration by product through 2031.
o.rationale: Software can automate ordering and reduce sales touches but cannot itself take title, extend trade credit, package product, deliver fuel or cylinders, manage returns, or bear product and contractual responsibility. Nonetheless this intermediary layer is more bypassable than bulk infrastructure: customers may contract directly with refiners or terminals and outsource transport separately. The base therefore preserves most operator need while allowing meaningful disintermediation.

caveats: No causal pass-through study for AI savings in petroleum wholesaling was found.
Nominal sales and gross margin can be dominated by fuel-price changes rather than retained productivity.
q.change_evidence: Customer- and route-level panels showing quoted versus realized spread, freight and surcharge treatment, contract repricing, gross profit per stop, bad debt, churn, delivery frequency, and contribution margin before and after verified workflow automation.
q.rationale: Automation savings can be retained through lower overhead, denser routes, fewer billing errors, improved credit discipline, and better sales coverage. Yet product prices are transparent and frequently reset, many accounts negotiate spreads or solicit bids, and manufacturers, terminals, and competing jobbers can pressure margins. Over five discounted years, a large share of pure clerical savings should pass through, while route-density, service-level, loss-prevention, and credit improvements are more defensible.

caveats: No representative longitudinal AI implementation study for 424720 was found.
Technical feasibility may exceed realized adoption at owner-operated firms with legacy systems.
rho.change_evidence: Cohort data from petroleum-distribution ERP, routing, cardlock, pricing, and telematics vendors reporting adoption, straight-through order rates, dispatch hours, route miles, invoice exceptions, errors, overrides, and verified headcount avoidance over 24 months.
rho.rationale: Within five years, firms can implement AI-supported quoting, order capture, dispatch assistance, route planning, customer messaging, invoice matching, credit document review, and sales prioritization using existing ERP, telematics, and communications systems. Constraints include fragmented price feeds, legacy software, thin IT teams, cybersecurity, product and tax complexity, customer-specific terms, and the need for people to approve hazardous-material delivery and credit exceptions.

s5.bridge: BizBuySell reports actual sold wholesale/distribution businesses nationwide from 2021 through 2025, including nondurable distributors, but not a 424720 transaction rate or eligible-firm denominator. I use the recurring sales and 199-day median marketing period as evidence of broad small-distributor liquidity, then bound the five-year probability for petroleum wholesalers after allowing for inventory finance, hazardous-product diligence, supply and brand approvals, environmental exposure, and route/customer concentration. Marketplace omissions bias activity downward, while its smaller successful listings and asset-sale mix can bias transferability upward.
caveats: No industry-specific owner-age or succession-rate evidence was found.
Broad marketplace transactions skew smaller than the target EBITDA band and omit private strategic deals.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of PitchBook, Axial, SBA 7(a), GF Data, broker, state fuel-license ownership-change, and supplier approval records matched to a verified denominator of eligible 424720 firms, with internal reorganizations and asset liquidations removed.
s5.rationale: Independent route and account books can transfer, and broad wholesale businesses transact regularly, but a five-year sale remains a minority outcome. Some owners will retain, internally succeed, merge without a qualifying control event, or close; other deals will fail because credit lines, supply agreements, or key customer relationships do not transfer.

principal_driver: Repeat ordering, pricing, dispatch, route, credit, and reconciliation workflows offer practical AI-enabled operating leverage around a necessary physical product flow.
principal_weakness: Commodity transparency, direct-sourcing risk, low labor intensity, and a small uncertain pool of transferable firms limit the size and durability of the opportunity.

caveats: S1 does not expose the claimed six-digit definition at the cited URL. This weakens lens and product-scope support for e and d5, but those inputs remain explicitly estimated or proxied and correcting the citation would not change the STRUCTURAL_OUT base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

The task-exposure evidence is occupation-level and mostly national or NAICS-424-wide rather than payroll-weighted 424720 task evidence. The a bridge states this limitation and gives plausible error directions.

S5 constrains one LP-gas dispensing activity but provides no evidence for rho=0.55 and only narrow evidence for o=0.81; the record honestly treats rho as ESTIMATE and o as PROXY and discloses the product-scope limitation.
S6 verifies large nominal sales changes but does not establish commodity-price causation, firm eligibility, or retained AI savings, so its use for e and q is contextual rather than calibrating.

S7 documents successful marketplace sales without a denominator, petroleum-specific population, or five-year transfer incidence. The s5 bridge and caveats disclose those limitations, but the 0.17 base remains judgment-heavy.

S8-S10 describe national gasoline, export, propane inventory, and production conditions rather than five-year volume handled by independent non-terminal wholesalers; d5 is therefore a broad proxy despite accurate source statistics.

Direct sourcing appears in both d5 and o, creating some downward double-counting risk between demand quantity and merchant-layer operator need. Removing that overlap would raise D, but the fixed low labor factor still leaves the base tier STRUCTURAL_OUT and the action unchanged.

sources_audited.note: The cited Census URL reopened, but the rendered page did not expose the claimed 424720 definition or examples; it showed the general NAICS interface and a query for 424.
The BLS NAICS 424 page reports the cited 2025 occupation counts, while clearly identifying the population as the broader nondurable-goods subsector.
BLS reports 1% projected growth for 2024-2034 and states that online sales are expected mostly to complement face-to-face selling while AI and chat bots may limit growth.
BLS reports 8% projected growth for 2024-2034 and continued strong business demand for delivery services.

OSHA supports the equipment safeguards and competent-attendant rule for vehicle LP-gas dispensing, but this narrow rule does not itself support a 55% implementation rate or operator need across all 424720 products.

Census supports the cited NAICS 4247 sales levels, 45.4% increase, and approximately 8,400-firm survey sample; the page does not attribute the increase to commodity prices or measure eligibility or savings retention.

BizBuySell reports the cited 2021-2025 sold-business benchmarks, including the 199-day median marketing period and nondurable-distributor median sale price; the bridge correctly disclaims an industry transfer rate.
EIA reports 2025 gasoline consumption of 8.9 million barrels per day, down 1% from 2024 and 4% from 2019, with continued near-term decline forecast.
EIA reports January 2026 clean-tanker refined-product exports of 6.3 million barrels per day, about 10% above January 2025, with diesel, gasoline, and LPG contributing.
The exact mixed-case URL was brittle, but its canonical lowercase form reopened the same EIA article and supported all cited inventory, household-heating, and production figures.

run_meta.title: Petroleum and Petroleum Products Merchant Wholesalers (except Bulk Stations and Terminals)

sources.supports: Defines 424720 as petroleum-product merchant wholesale distribution except from bulk liquid storage facilities and lists gasoline, lubricating oil and grease, bottled LP gas, and fuel oil merchant wholesalers as examples.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 424720 Petroleum and Petroleum Products Merchant Wholesalers (except Bulk Stations and Terminals)

sources.supports: Reports broader-subsector 2025 employment including 269,740 nontechnical sales representatives, 174,610 heavy truck drivers, 162,830 material movers, 54,000 technical sales representatives, and 43,040 shipping/receiving/traffic clerks.
sources.title: Merchant Wholesalers, Nondurable Goods: NAICS 424

sources.supports: Projects overall wholesale/manufacturing sales-representative employment to grow 1% from 2024 to 2034; says online sales mostly complement face-to-face selling and AI and chatbots may limit employment growth.
sources.title: Wholesale and Manufacturing Sales Representatives

sources.supports: Projects 8% employment growth from 2024 to 2034 and says business demand for local delivery remains strong, while e-commerce and demand for convenient delivery support driver needs.
sources.title: Delivery Truck Drivers and Driver/Sales Workers

sources.supports: Specifies LP-gas equipment, pump, valve, hose, emergency-shutoff, and dispensing safeguards and requires a competent attendant to remain at an LP-gas dispenser during the entire vehicle transfer operation.
sources.title: 29 CFR 1910.110 Storage and Handling of Liquefied Petroleum Gases

sources.supports: Reports NAICS 4247 sales of $2,089.5 billion in 2022, up 45.4% from $1,436.8 billion in 2021, illustrating the scale and commodity-price sensitivity of petroleum-wholesale nominal revenue; notes the survey samples about 8,400 wholesale firms.
sources.title: 2022 Annual Wholesale Trade Survey

sources.supports: Reports actual wholesale/distribution business sales from 2021-2025, including $600,000 median sale price, $1,378,849 median revenue, $236,484 median owner earnings, 199 median days on market, and $573,500 median sale price for nondurable distributors.
sources.title: Wholesale and Distribution Business Valuation Benchmarks

sources.supports: Reports US motor-gasoline consumption of 8.9 million barrels per day in 2025, 1% below 2024 and 4% below 2019, and forecasts continued decline in 2026 and 2027.
sources.title: Increasing Fuel Efficiency Leads to Decreasing Gasoline Consumption

sources.supports: Reports refined-product exports on clean tankers of 6.3 million barrels per day in January 2026, about 10% above January 2025, with diesel, gasoline, and LPG contributing to growth.
sources.title: Maritime Exports of Petroleum Products Increased in January 2026

sources.supports: Reports 103 million barrels of propane inventory for the week ending September 26, 2025, 13 million above the five-year average; says propane is the main heating fuel for about 5% of US homes and gas-plant propane production grew 5% in the first seven months of 2025.
sources.title: U.S. Propane Inventories Are Well Stocked Heading into the Winter Heating Season

title: Petroleum and Petroleum Products Merchant Wholesalers (except Bulk Stations and Terminals)
